event: Assumption or Apotheosis of Saint Catherine of Alexandria
symbols: angels carrying the saint — divine honor and transport to heavenly glory
white robes — purity and the garments of the redeemed
crescent moon near the horizon — the passage from earthly darkness to heavenly light
twilight sky — the transition between earth and heaven, mortality and eternity
musical angels — the heavenly court celebrating the martyr's arrival in paradise
description: A dramatic wide-format painting showing a group of angels carrying a female figure (Saint Catherine) through the sky. The saint lies recumbent in flowing white robes, supported by multiple winged figures in colorful drapery. Additional angels fly alongside, some carrying musical instruments. They soar above a vast landscape at twilight, with a crescent moon visible near the horizon. The deep blue sky transitions to warm sunset tones at the lower edge. The composition conveys a sense of dynamic movement from left to right across the sky.
theological_description: This painting depicts the legendary transport of Saint Catherine of Alexandria's body by angels to Mount Sinai after her martyrdom. Catherine, a 4th-century Christian scholar and princess, was tortured on a spiked wheel (which broke) and then beheaded for her faith under Emperor Maxentius. While Catherine is not a biblical figure, her apotheosis illustrates biblical promises about the faithful departed. Paul writes: 'For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command... and the dead in Christ will rise first. Then we who are alive... will be caught up together with them in the clouds to meet the Lord in the air, and so we will always be with the Lord' (1 Thessalonians 4:16-17). The transport to Mount Sinai — where Moses received the Law — creates a profound theological connection between the old covenant given to Moses and the new covenant for which Catherine died. The angels carrying her body fulfill Psalm 91:11-12: 'For he will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways. On their hands they will bear you up.' Jesus himself cited this psalm (Matthew 4:6), and the scene affirms that God's care for his saints extends beyond death into glory.

scripture_refs.reason: The dead in Christ rising and being caught up in the air — the saint carried heavenward by angels
Angels commanded to guard and bear up the faithful — angels carrying Catherine's body
Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord — the honor shown to the martyr's remains
You will be hated and put to death for my name's sake — Catherine's martyrdom for Christian faith
